[IMAGE:0]It's just after midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. (I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The picture? A leatherbound book. In the traditions of the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose and the orders and systems related to it, the book is the emblem and working tool of elemental air.  This is one of the distinctive habits of the FHR, since many other traditions assign something else -- usually but not always a dagger -- as the working tool of air. Dr. Juliet Ashley, the founder of the order that evolved into the FHR, made the change for two reasons. First, in her time and in ours, there were endless squabbles over whether the wand corresponded to fire and the dagger to air, or vice versa, and she wanted to put an end to that. Second, for a variety of reasons, she didn't want to include an iron blade in the toolkit of the working mage. The book as the working tool of air solves both problems, and works well in practice.

comments.content: JMG - what's your opinion on the "second coming" of Christ?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It's among the beliefs of a religion to which I don't belong. The claim that some important figure of the past will miraculously show up again at some time in the future -- usually it's supposed to be any day now, of course -- is very widespread in world mythology; Zoroaster, the founder of the Zoroastrian religion, is supposed to return someday, and so is King Arthur and the Holy Roman Emperor Frederick I and any number of other monarchs; Muslims wait for the coming of the Mahdi, Hindus for the coming of Kalki, the next avatar of Vishnu, and Tibetan Buddhists for the appearance of Rigden Jyepo, the warrior king of Shambhala -- well, the list goes on and on.

People have been predicting such things for many thousands of years. None of the predictions have ever turned out to be correct. Of course every religion claims that theirs is the true prophecy and all the others are fakes, but from my outside perspective, they're all variations on a common theme. Take that theme as a symbol and it has a lot of meaning; take it as a literal truth, and you're setting yourself up for the same disappointment as all those New Agers who were convinced that the whole world would change on December 21, 2012.

replies.title: Re: 2nd coming
replies.content:
You will probably have better things to do, but a work of fiction that comes to mind is "the Gospel according to Lucas Gavilán". The premise of the novel is that we Christians are as deluded in precisely *how* the Christ is to come to the world again as the 1st century Jews were about the coming of their Messiah.

In the here and now a Jesús López is born again to a virgin, Maria; he grows up to become a just, proper man with leadership gifts. He gathers and organizes followers, goes around for several years teaching and doing wonders in the central valley of Mexico, only to end up murdered by the Policia Judicial (a now dismantled Mexican law enforcement agency that had no exact equivalent in USA, but you can think of them as Dark_Side_FBI).

As methylethyl said further down, the actual literality of the second coming has little meaning on how you live your life because you will not continue to live afterwards (you'd skip death, at least according to some lines of thought, but that is more or less irrelevant). But if you think of the values showcased in the events of Revelations book, and how are you going to align yourself according to those values, that is something worth spending some nice evenings at.

replies.title: Re: 2nd coming

Here again, though, I could read your apocalyptic scripture, or any of hundreds of others, and I don't have too many spare evenings as it is!

Everything that is beautiful and truth and useful can be turned into a mockery that is ugly, lying and worthless. The doctrine of the second coming is not different.

When a stranger (the Serpent) comes to tell you that your family (God) wants to keep you (Adam and Eve) dumb and obedient, it is not because they want to set you free. Do you think we are on our way to bigger and better things now? I'd say we replaced our tyrannical overlords of yesteryear with a bunch of shameless, hungrier tyrannical overlords.

The path to freedom is slow and full of hardships. May you traverse it with vigor and in good company.

comments.title: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
comments.content:
Good evening,

I hope the coming change of season is bringing everyone hoped-for changes in weather and situation, and minimal trouble.

To Share: A bit more personal this week, I reflect a bit on what I like and appreciate about Texas and its culture, but also why I'm not sure it's the right place for my family to put down roots for the future: https://jpowellrussell.com/#texas

To Ask: I just got your updated edition of Coelbren in the mail, and I'm especially intrigued by the idea of Gematria for alphabets other than Hebrew and Greek (unsurprisingly, I'm most interested in its application to the Runes). Do you, JMG, or anyone else here, have any recommendations for sources on Gematria? Since the application seems straightforward enough, I suppose where guidance might be most helpful is on the assignment of numerical values to letters, if it's any more complicated than "do 1-10, then do multiples of 10 up to 100, then do multiples of 100 up to 1000, until you run out of letters." That being said, I welcome anything that might be helpful, gematria-wise.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone here for all you do to make this such a worthwhile space.

My blessings to any who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content: Kieren Barry's book The Greek Qabalah is a good resource. You might also want to look into theories of the historical origin of the runes; last I heard they'd been traced back to an archaic Italian alphabet that was derived from Greek, so it may be possible to determine the gematria values of the runes by taking those of their corresponding Greek letters.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
Hmm, that's an intriguing thought! I have, but haven't yet read in detail, Stephen Pollington's Runes: Literacy in the Germanic Iron Age, which is a fairly recent and thorough survey of the historical development and use of the Runes. From what I remember of a skim from there, and the other sources I've read/watched/listened to, a Mediterranean model is nearly certain. Whether you pick Latin, Etruscan, or Greek as that model has to do with what weight you put on different sources and how far back you think the influence happened - Greek makes great sense if you think the Elder Futhark developed among the Goths bumping into the northern frontiers of the proto-Byzantines of the eastern empire, Latin makes more sense for a later influence from Germans serving in the legions, and Etruscan makes sense if you posit an earlier influence centered on the Alps.

Anyhow, all of that is interesting and all, but what it leads to: you mentioned in a reply to a comment last week or the week before that most numerological systems follow the order of the alphabet they're associated with. Every version of the Runes we have has a rather well-established order (other than the last two runes in the Elder Futhark, where there's some variation). I'm guessing this will produce a big disparity between "assign number by Futhark order" versus "equate runes to Greek letters and use the Greek values." For example, Fehu is the first letter of the Elder Futhark, and so would be given "1" on a pure ordering basis, but would be equivalent to Phi in the Greek alphabet, which has a gematria value of 500, so rather different!

Since this doesn't seem like an area where experimentation would be easy/obvious, any thoughts on whether to prefer the Greek-equivalent (or Latin, or whatever else) over the Futhark-order assignment? And to the degree that it does need to be settled "experimentally," what would be the method of evaluation? Meditation on the results of doing gematria each way?

Thanks much,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
First, I'm not at all sure that Fehu would equal Phi. The sound value is the same but the letter shape is not, and it's the shapes that seem to have been borrowed from some Mediterranean source, and may have come with numerical values attached.

Second, if in fact the runes were assigned new numerical meanings, it's quite possible that the first aett was the numbers 1-9 with one left out, the second the numbers 10-90 with one left out, and the third the numbers 100-900 with one left out; the Greek alphabet worked that way, for example.

Third, Norse mythology has important numbers. If you can find a system in which Valhalla adds to 432, for example, you're on to something -- iirc that's the number of doors it had. That gives you a way of testing any given theory. Does Sleipnir add to 8, or 80, or 800? That kind of thing.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
I really like this suggestion of finding gematria numbers for the Runes within Norse mythology! This seems a much more meaningful, and hence powerful approach. Thor's hall Bilskirnir has 540 doors--a number that could be used for Thurizaz. Eleven for Laguz, to reflect the Elivagar or 11 rivers of power that flow from Hvergelmir. For Dagaz, maybe 90 (degrees) for the angle of high noon with respect to where one stands on Earth.

Null or zero for Isa, the state of no movement. One for Kenaz, the fire of the 'one life'--Kenaz is not only the torch for the nobles in the feasting hall, but the light of life that burns within the child in the womb, the 'feasting hall' where life begins. Thus Ice / Isa = 0 and Fire / Kenaz = 1, for the first polarities and a binary system of coming-into-being. Then 2 for Mannaz, human beings, the children of Fire and Ice. Mannaz's shape can be seen as the two Ravens facing each other beak to beak: Thought and Memory shaping our human powers.

Oh well, just ideas! The search itself for the gematria numbers seems like a very enjoyable pursuit when based on numbers that are meaningful to the Runes and to our mythology.

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
Interesting! Yes, you could pursue things that way.

The traditional approach in those cultures that are known to have used gematria was a little more complicated, though. If that's what the Norse were up to, the name of the hall Bilskirnir, written in runes and added up according to their gematria, would equal 540. Other numbers in Norse mythology assigned to specific names would work the same way. It might well be possible -- though this is a kind of riddle I'm not good at solving -- to take those clues and reconstruct the entire system from them.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content: Thank you for these! One of the many research projects I have on the "to get to" list is a deep dive on Germanic number symbolism, and your thoughts here give some helpful threads to follow.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
Thanks for these!

1) Hmm, that's an excellent point I hadn't considered - I'm so used to thinking of letters as the sound they make that I didn't consider the visual shape.

2) That's rather interesting, especially with the significance of 9 in Germanic lore.

3) Thank you, that's some helpful guidance as I start exploring.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
I took another look at the section on the origins of the Runes in Pollington's Runes: Literacy in the Germanic Iron Age. Pretty much the only folks who argue for a sui generis origin in Scandinavia do so for political reasons, and scholars accept a clear influence from some Mediterranean script, but which one has been debated over the past couple of hundred years, with various reasons to support Roman, "Northern Italic" (a family of loosely-related scripts that included Etruscan), or Greek.

His survey of the evidence leads him to believe that the Elder Futhark was devised by Germanic speakers who were familiar with the Roman alphabet and literacy, and found the concepts useful for one reason or another (increased prestige, administration of larger estates, etc), but also did not want for one reason or another to adopt the Roman script "as is" (cultural distinctiveness, suspicion of Roman control, etc). He draws parallels with the Germanic adoption of some Roman equipment and tactics well before they were willing to fight for the Romans.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content: Regarding the Mediterranean model for the runes, it might be interesting to look up the Southwest Script. Found in the southwest Iberian peninsula,it's a very old script, not completely deciphered yet, and some of its characters bear similarities to some of the Nordic runes. Coincidentally, or maybe not, the people who are thought to have used it (the Conii) are also thought to have been the builders of a number of very old (like 2000 years older than Stonehenge) stone circles that can be found roughly on the same area.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
Greek and Hebrew gamatria go by nines: nine units (1-9), nine decades (10-90), and nine hundreds (100-900) in Greek, the first four hundreds (100-400) in Hebrew. This, of course, is our familiar system of numbers--what mathematicians call "numbers to the base 10."

The 24 runes, however, are grouped by eights (the three aettir). So Runic gematria, if it ever existed (and why should it not have?), would more naturally have been based on a system of "numbers to the base 9." There's no reason to think that such a system of counting "to the bsse 9" was ever in practical, every-day use during the historic period of the Germanic peoples. Yet 9 is clearly a (the?) most sacred number in Germanic Odinic lore: the nine worlds (connected by Yggdrasil), the nine points of the valknut, the nine days and nights that Odin hung on the windswept tree in agony to win his knowledge of the runes themselves, the twice-nine spell-songs that only Odin knows, and probably several other examples that aren't coming to mind right now.

This makes Runic gematria far harder to reconstruct, or to create anew. None of us use numbers to the base 9 today, not even professional mathematicians (so far as I know), and it will take a major intellectual effort to even wrap our minds around their use, or to come up with workable names for such numbers. Runic gematria, if someone ever manages the feat of reconstructing it, won't map itself one-to-one wih the more familiar systems still in esoteric use today.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content: And I just read our host's excellent comment on this thread.  I had not thought of the possibility of testing one's proposed Runic gematria against the important numbers of Norse mythology.  He's quite right (IMHO) about that!  Almost certainly there is more to those particular numbers than we have realized.  I'll have to mull this over and see what I can come up with.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
Hmm, that's quite intriguing. I know Base 8 arithmetic is fairly commonly used in computer science, but I've also never heard of base 9.

There are various Runic-adjacent cipher systems that use the number of branches to indicate which Rune was enchiphered. I wonder if there are any mathematical significances if you examine them (not that I'm particularly well suited for that line of investigation).

Thanks very much!

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
You could try building yourself a base-9 simple abacus and play around with the numbers.

Here is a page that describes the Russian base-10 "schoty" abacus (scroll down):

http://retrocalculators.com/abacus.htm

Now you got me curious too.

- Cicada Grove

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Texas and Request for Gematria Resources
replies.content:
So, after I got home, I pulled down Edred Thorsson's Futhark to see if he has anything to say on the topic, and unsurprisingly, he does. He simply assigns the number 1-24 to each rune in its traditional order (sticklers will note that he prefers the ordering where Othala is last rather than Dagaz).

For all interested, here's the brief section on numerical symbolism from pg 102-104:


Numerical criteria play an important role in the loading and working of rune tines, and they are often critical in other types of rune magic. In the ancient inscriptions we sometimes find that the vitki has in some way sacrificed linguistic clarity for numerical (or ideographic) potency. This is done by leaving out staves (especially vowels), or by adding or doubling them.

As noted at the beginning of this book, number is one of the three keys to each rune. The commentary given under each rune is also pertinent to the symbolism of its number. Indeed much of the interpretation is drawn from numerical criteria. Strong examples of this would be the H-rune (9) - the Nine Worlds of Yggdrasill; and the J-rune (12) - the twelve months of the solar year. It is necessary only to give the broad outlines of runic numerology here; the true vitki will find the right roads to further power.

The numerical values of inscriptions, tines, and magical formulas place the power of the runes in various "spheres of working" and also draw on the power inherent in that number for their working. Often it is best to aim for a harmonious and broad-based sphere of working to lend maximal overall power to the ideographic and linguistic form of the formula. These formulas also may modify or adjust the overall power of the whole. Since every formula and tine works on various levels simultaneously, a general rule of thumb would be the more levels of meaning you can pack into the least amount of space, and the more cryptic you can make it, the more effective the magic will be. This is important in the shaping as well as the interpretation of talismans.

Runic number formulas are analyzed in two ways: (1) the rune count, that is, the number of staves in the formula; and (2) the total of the numerical values of each rune in the formula (as in gematria). These numbers are then broken down into their multiples im order to further analyze their powers. A simple example of this process is shown in Figure 4.10: Rune count 8 (multiple: 2x4); runic total: 66 (multiple: 6x11). This formula is found on ancient talismans, and forms an incantation of great ideographic, phonetic, and numerological power.

[Laguz Uruz Wunjo Ansuz Tiwaz Uruz Wunjo Ansuz]

Figure 4.10: Runic number formula "luwatuwa"

Either or both of these systems may be used. The meaning of these numbers is twofold. They indicate the sphere in which the formula is to work and the power by which it works.

There are several "numbers of power" in both of these systems on which the beginning vitki could concentrate. For the rune count, the numbers 1 through 24 are all powerful, and imbue the formula with the force of the rune of that number. Also, the use of any twenty-four runes in an inscription provides a broad bases of power and invokes the force of the whole rune row into the formula. To a twenty-four-fold rune count, the number eight and its multiples (and indeed twenty-four and its multiples) may be added to maintain the whole harmony of power while intensifying its force. The multiple of the rune count also modifies the runic potency in subtle and ingenious ways. These are common patterns in ancient inscriptions.

On the second level, that of the numerical total of the runes, there are many possibilities and numbers of power, which direct the runic force in specific directions and give them special magical characteristics. Of course, the sums 1 through 24indicate the sphere where that particular rune is at work. Prime numbers are especially powerful and express a tremendous amount of will. Whatever the runic total might be, it is through its multiple factors that the root force of the number is revealed. Multiples of three, and especially of nine, are powerful in operations dealing with magical forces working on many levels at once, including the earthly realm. Multiples of ten are especially forceful when the intent is to cause a change in the manifest world of Midhgardhr. Twelve and its multiples also are potent in this regard but have a more prolonged and enduring effect. The number thirteen and its multiples are the most universal numbers of power. A vast number of runic inscriptions manifest this numerological pattern. The number is indicative of universal potency and contains the mystery of eihwaz as the world-tree (9) and the three realms (3) in the ontological oneness of Ginnungagap (1). The number by which the "master number" is multiplied further modifies and directs the overall force of the formula according to its runic nature.

All of these principles may be used when constructing rune tines and rituals; however, they need not dominate the form of the operation. Let intuition and natural inclination be your guide. Vitkar may totally dispense with numerical considerations, and their results will in no way diminish. The correct use of runic numerology is an art in itself and one that needs to be supplemented with a large dose of Nordic lore to be completely effective. The study and analysis of inscriptions fashioned by our forebears should be the guiding light in our efforts. It also should be pointed out that the old Germanic attitude toward the concept of number was quite different from that held by their neighbors to the south. To the Pythagorean and Gnostic mystics, number came to be the *arche* (rule) of all things, but to the vitkar, number was only one among three equal expressions of the same holy mystery embodied by a rune. While the Gnostics and Pythagoreans tended to look at number as a way of measuring and distinguishing one thing from another, the vitkar saw them as points of connection and interrelation in a cosmos forever in a state of ebb and flow.

Good Morning Sir and Druid,

As continuation of last week's comment regarding the translation of Astrometric RA and Declination coordinates into Ecliptic coordinates for the purpose of further conversion to Astrological positions, I found additional resources to this end.

https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/horizons/

JPL, as part of its functions in serving the planetary science community, has within its web page an ephemeris tool for all kinds of celestial bodies; planets, asteroids, and for our purposes, comets. As it is a scientific publication, all times are in UT.

For our astrological purposes, the tool will need to be configured, entering the target body, in our case C/2023 P1 (it's sensitive to capitalization and spacing), the date range and time units, and most importantly, the Table settings, of which the relevant one to our needs are #31 Observer Ecliptic Longitude and Latitude.

https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/horizons/app.html

Secondly, I was reading the November 2012 issue of Sky and Telescope, within which is an article describing the history of eclipse predictions, with focus on the development of eclipse maps from the Renaissance to the modern day.

Interestingly, part of the development was spurred by a series of eclipses crisscrossing the British Isles in 1715, 1724, 1737, 1748, 1764. With so many eclipses affecting the British Isles, this may provide a test case for mundane astrology eclipse predictions.

comments.title: hallucinogens, religion, and maybe the druids?
comments.content:
So in my understanding, there seems to be a deep connection between hallucinogens and religion let it be ayahuasca, peyote, or the Yew Tree (the last of the bunch the only one I'm as educated on as I should be).

Hallucinogens (or maybe other drugs) also can be made from poisonous items and I know the 3 holy plants to the druids are Oak, Misltetoe, and Vervain. (At least 2 of which being somewhat poisonous to humans)

Do you think these plants might be holy to the druids because there is a special ratio (among other things) that when combined produces a hallucinogenic, and with this brew the druidic religion revealed to people?

replies.title: Re: hallucinogens, religion, and maybe the druids?
replies.content:
Um, well, to start with, I disagree with your basic assumption. Most religions have nothing to do with hallucinogens, because anything you can do with drugs, you can do more effectively and with better results using the internal chemistry of your own brain -- that's what spiritual practice is meant to do, you know. It's just that spiritual practice takes work.

I know of no evidence that the ancient Druids used any kind of hallucinogens in their practices. What little we know about their work, mostly by way of fragmentary practices in later Celtic bardic traditions, suggests that they practiced meditation and other spiritual disciplines. In the modern Druid tradition, the plants you've named are important for symbolic reasons only.

To ask, I just finished the Hounds of Actaeon by Mauricio Loza. It's about the magic spell cast on us by marketing and advertisement. It ends with:

Lest the fog lift from our eyes and we wake up in this nightmare!

I imagined waking up between tentacled beings. Or learning my evaluation during a bad news talk at work. Or returning home after an infatuating night out.

Given the context of the book I suppose fog means the magic of advertisements. The quote suggests that you can "wake up" from that spell. And indeed, you can turn off the TV and quit social media. But then it seems to me, would you not just "wake up" into a different spell? You can wake into an intellectual or sensual fog, a fog composed by others or by yourself, a fog where magical rituals work and one where they do not. You could even wake up several times and end up in the fog where you started.

What can you say about what triggers "waking up"?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
No idea what other triggers to 'waking up' might be but I'll offer my experience. What triggered me was seeing the complete absurdity of what I was being sold. I was about eight and needed new shoes so my parents took me to get some. At the shop, I was puzzled by some of the designs and also by what I'd seen people wearing on the street. What seemed really odd was that so many people were wearing very similar designs, most of them impractical and ugly (honestly, how are you going to climb trees wearing those?). I remember my dad explaining that it was because of 'fashion' and he said that followers of fashion want to look the same. That seemed one of the most ridiculous ideas I'd ever come across and, more than 50 years later, I'm still as baffled.

Ridicule is said to be a very effective breaker of attractions, whatever they may be.

replies.title: (no subject)

For example, the mental flower of "love of commodities" loses its vitality, and the flower of "love of football" sprouts. This could be triggered by an external event, like unavailability of commodities, or by an internal one, like enjoying the group feeling in a football stadium. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Oh, it's not infinite. The road is long and winding, but it does have a destination.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't remember any specific awakening event, but by this time in my life I find advertisements and marketing in any medium nothing but amusing. Characters in ad vignettes have strange facial expressions and strange speech patterns. Social interactions (and many other fun activities) are depicted as weirdly frenetic instead of pleasant. People act stupid, such as needing the right brand of paper towel to stop the spilled liquid before it reaches some small precious object, instead of just picking up the endangered object. Sales gimmicks are confessions of desperation (e.g. "if they're selling out fast, why did you spend money to run this ad?"). Emotionally manipulative music, sounds, and images might as well be blocks of text reading things like "now feel frightened... now feel relieved that a solution is at hand."

While avoiding being exposed to ads at all is more certain to be effective, it's hard to manage it all the time. I'm pretty sure active-thinking ad resistance is something that could be taught and learned.

comments.title: Discursive contemplation and Resonance
comments.content:
Discursive Contemplation and Resonance

Greetings, esteemed JMG and all!

A couple days after our conversation about discursive contemplation last week I had one of those smack-yourself-on-the-forehead realizations: I’ve actually been doing this for a long time, and the method is a time-honored one in many religious traditions. I just wasn’t thinking of it in terms of discursive meditation, but it is basically the same approach, just focused on the spiritual rather than the mental plane.

So to backtrack from last week: I can offer some simple guidelines to get started here, as several people were asking. The preparation that JMG describes for discursive meditation in his writing is perfect for this type of work, as well.

As part of your preparation, instead of choosing a ‘seed for thought’ to pursue discursively, you take a ‘seed for contemplation’ about a Deity, a mythic event, a description or metaphor of some otherworldly location or process. This could involve a sentence describing a Deity’s attributes or actions, for example, or it could be an artistic portrayal of the Deity or of a metaphysical location like the World-Tree, a poem or song, a mythic or religious symbol, or the account of someone else’s vision or insight.

But there’s another step to the process here because this seed needs to be turned into an image or symbol, if it isn’t already in that form. It’s better not to use words except for symbolic words like the Deity’s name. With words, it’s too easy to stay tuned to the mental plane; we need symbols or images here, including non-visual images like sound, sensation, or ‘impression.’ Even scent or taste are sometimes used, such as incense or metaphorical honey or ‘manna from heaven.’

Rather than ‘thinking’ about this image-seed, you use it as a pathway to the presence of the Deity or spiritual location or state of being that you are seeking to connect with. Here we get back to the connection with the heart I was writing about last week: you follow this path / thread / seed by seeking with your heart more than with your mind, expanding your heart-energy outward and opening it up, recognizing that among its myriad talents the heart is a sensory organ, a generator and receiver of subtle resonances on multiple planes of being.

Here’s a Heathen example of contacting Thor contemplatively / immersively. I choose his characteristic ‘thunder’ as a seed image for this—a sound-image. Contemplatively (ideally during a real thunderstorm), I immerse myself in thunder, surround myself with everything that thunder is: it shakes my being, its vibrations reshape everything inside and outside myself, pushing out stagnancy, bringing in new waves of power. I am a vibration, Thor is a vibration, we draw into resonance with each other. I open my heart and Thor’s thunder roars through. There’s a voice in that thunder: what does it utter? The heart can sense these wordless resonances and transform them into meaning. Thunder recedes into a murmur on some unseen horizon, vibrations turn back into my physical being, renewed by resonance with Thor’s thunder.

I’m forced to describe this experience with words and concepts in order to communicate it, but that is done after the event is over. Contemplation isn’t a guided meditation that is designed in advance. Guided meditation is for meditation, not contemplation. Instead, we throw ourselves along that thread to Deity, into the unknown, and see what happens when we get there. During the event, we are submerged in the direct experience of Being, not in our mental-verbal description of it. Our attempt to describe it afterwards is a pale reflection of the experience, often better captured in art, poetry, music, or sacred dance.

Here’s a closing example of the process. Long ago, I took a line about the Goddess Frigg from the Poetic Edda as my ‘seed’, combined with a painting of her weaving the clouds. The line was spoken by Freya, saying: “Of orlog Frigg has full knowledge, I think, although she does not speak (of it) herself.” I wondered why Frigg doesn’t speak of orlog, so I turned this phrase into an ‘impression of Frigg’s wordlessness,’ combined with the image of her weaving clouds, i.e. weaving mysteries while not speaking of them, and followed those threads to her.

When I came back I wrote the following poem-prayer. Over years of working with it and with the Deities, I’ve come to believe that ‘resonances, resonance-fractals, and fields of interwoven resonances’ are to the spiritual plane what ‘words, sentences, concepts’ are to the mental plane, and what material substance is to the plane of matter. And not only sound-resonances (this may be where the ‘choirs of angels around the throne of God’ imagery comes from). Also fractals as shape-resonances, orchestras of color-tonalities, symphonies of scent-shadows, resonances of phenomena for which we have no sensory analogs. The experience behind this poem-prayer, and Frigg's ongoing response to it, started me on that exploration and has since shaped all my spiritual experience.

Holy Frigg, Norn-wise,
You know no tongue with which to tell
What is and what shall be,
To sort the spinning strands of possibility
Into a span of words.
Yet with your spindle and your well-strung loom,
You weave the airy clouds
And send the winds to shape them,
Writing your wordless wisdom-runes
Across the ever-changing valleys of the sky.
Teach us, Lady, to heed
The wisdom that lies beyond all words:
Echoes that resound
After words have fled.

comments.title: Astrological Curiosity
comments.content:
JMG, I’d like to share a particular theory in vedic astrology (which I am strong in) to find out if it has a parallel in western astrology (which I am weak in).

A particularly bright young vedic astrologer, by name of Abhigya Ananda, recently posted a video about India’s future. A key portion of the video discusses Great Conjunctions between Jupiter and Saturn. According to Abhigya Ananda, there is a cycle of Great Conjunctions that are 400 years in length: these conjunctions presage global changes in power and wealth. For example, the last conjunction of this type took place in the early 1600s, which triggered multiple decades of political transformation in Europe, India and quite a few other places around the world. After the transformational period we had an era which had four key features: (1) creation of the stock market, (2) European domination of the world, (3) widespread systematic use of the slave trade, and (4) the global development and distribution of sugar. Each era has its ‘pros’ and ‘cons’.

The latest Great Conjunction was in 2020: it was the closest great conjunction in 800 years and is therefore predicted to produce the greatest transformation since the early 13th century. Based on astrology, the next couple decades are going to be a rough ride socially and economically for the inhabitants of Planet Earth; this will be followed by a new ‘world order’ in which India and African nations will dominate the new 400-year era. (for anyone in the group who likes videos and is interested in the subject, , check out minutes 10:10 to 16:30 in the following: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lztmuCveRcM)

History is a bit like a Rorschach test in which historians will see cycles according to their predilections and biases (the fourth turning, the 250-year cycle, etc.). I am therefore curious to see if the 400-year cycle of Great Conjunctions is interpreted similarly in Western astrology.

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Astrological Curiosity
replies.content: I am now studying Vedic astrology in a lineage, but still learning the foundations. I also have a background in Renaissance and modern astrology, and yes, the cycle of Jupiter and Saturn is very important for western astrology. In the tropical system, we've just entered a 200 year period of this conjunction happening in tropical air signs, which has a lot of significance, and there's been a lot of talk about it by astrologers such as Chris Brennan and Austin Coppock.

replies.title: Re: Astrological Curiosity
replies.content:
Thanks, Isaac!

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Astrological Curiosity
replies.content:
Well, to some extent. I posted at length about the grand mutation (to give it its Western label) here, back when it happened:

https://www.ecosophia.net/the-grand-mutation-an-astrological-interlude/

In the Western system the great conjunctions that matter are the ones that take place when the trigon of conjunctions shifts decisively into a new element -- the element of Air in 2020, for example. The first conjunction in Fire, which is the one that took place in 1604, inaugurates a cycle of 800-odd years, and is considered the most important of all; the elemental shifts thereafter (1842, 2020, and 2219, for example) mark very significant inflection points within that cycle. Notice that India's struggle for independence began with the first great rising not long after the 1842 grand mutation, and the last traces of dependence on the West were thrown aside not all that long before 2020. Now we've passed another inflection point and the world is changing very rapidly...

replies.title: Re: Astrological Curiosity
replies.content:
Many thanks, JMG!

Ron M

comments.content:
JMG,

I always appriciate the insights I get into magic and paganism by reading your writing. I was wondering if I could pick your brain about my own religion's tendency of coveribg things?

1. In Judaism, we bake sacred bread every friday, and there is a tradition of covering it, blessing the wine, and only then uncovering it to bless the Challah. It occured to me a pagan might view this as an offering, which I understand sometimes takes the form of a bread cake of sort.

Do you know of any traditions where an offering is covered before a ceremony? What would be the reason?

2. In judaism all observent men and married women are expected to cover their heads at all times. In your own personal view, do you think insisting on covering the head at all times as spiritualy detremental?

Thank you as always for your thoutful answers.

replies.title: Re: Religions and covering things
replies.content:
Not JMG, but you might be interested in this video:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zpbaZXRLWgE

Which features a Coptic priest giving a tour of his church, explaining some of the ceremony. The eucharistic elements are tied up in a cloth as part of this.

replies.title: Re: Religions and covering things
replies.content:
Dear Anon,

I cannot comment on the symbolism of covering things (personally I always thought it was to keep the bread hot while desperately trying to corral all the children to the table ;), but lighting the candles and making kiddush and motzi are very obviously an offering. You light a candle and thank HaShem (in some cases you sing a song, Lecha Dodi, invoking the feminine presence of HaShem into the house), then you bless wine which is well known as HaShem's preferred beverage, and then you bless the bread, calling it lechem ha'aretz--literally "the flesh of the earth". And then you share the food and drink, including the deity in your meal.

Of course it is unfashionable to view the erev Shabbat ritual this way in current Jewish thought, but any outside observer would see this as the obvious offering it is.

-Ms. "Just An Ordinary Jewish Housewife" Krieger

replies.title: Re: Religions and covering things
replies.content:
1) I'm not at all familiar with other examples of this, but there's doubtless a good reason for it; traditional observances of that sort are never random.

2) There's an intriguing distinction between faiths that keep the head covered and faiths that do the opposite. Judaism is a head-covering faith; compare it to those faiths that have monks, who shave the tops of their heads to let the light in. (Then you have Catholicism, which has male monastics shave their heads and female monastics keep their heads permanently covered!) As a Druid, I assume that Nature knows what she's doing, so I encourage men to let the sun shine in through their bald spots but don't recommend doing anything artificial to make that happen.

(I'm pretty sure that what's behind it is the differing effects of light and darkness on the photosensitive tissues of the pineal gland -- the human skull and brain, after all, are transparent to longwave red light -- but that's a hypothesis for another day.)

comments.title: stoicism and gods
comments.content:
Hi JMG & commentariat,

I am thinking a lot about this quote by Marcus Aurelius:
"Live a good life. If there are gods and they are just, then they will not care how devout you have been, but will welcome you based on the virtues you have lived by. If there are gods, but unjust, then you should not want to worship them. If there are no gods, then you will be gone, but will have lived a noble life that will live on in the memories of your loved ones"

I feel that according to this, the gods don't interfere much in our lives, but it has been mentioned several times here on the blog that you can pray to and interact with a deity of your choice. Is stoic philosophy at odds with occult philosophy here, or am I not getting it right?
-Tom

replies.title: Re: stoicism and gods
replies.content:
In my experience with gods, they largely seem to obey the first rule which most spiritual interactions of a positive kind respect. They don't interfere directly without permission, athough sometimes reach out if they see you struggling, hence the need to build a relationship. After all if we're here to grow and be individuals shouldn't higher entities respect that and I'd be suspicious of a god who didn't lol just because I'm bigger than my animals doesn't mean I shouldnt respect them etc.

I like that quote.

replies.title: Re: stoicism and gods
replies.content: Marcus Aurelius was certainly entitled to his opinion, but Stoicism isn't a scriptural tradition -- the fact that he wrote that doesn't mean that all Stoics, or most Stoics, or many Stoics agree with it. Stoicism is a disciplined confrontation with the hard realities of the human condition. That being the case, no two Stoics are going to come to the same conclusions about much of anything -- and that is exactly as it should be.

replies.title: Re: stoicism and gods
replies.content:
I don't necessarily think this quote implies that the gods don't interfere much in our lives. It certainly allows for that possibility, but it also allows for the possibility of more active virtuous gods.

I think what it implies is a way to challenge the more dogmatic aspects of religion. If you find your religious doctrine is compelling you towards less virtuous acts, it would point you to trust your own measure of virtue rather than the dogma of your religion.

2. The different types of Samadhi (“meditative absorption mind-state”) were clarified a bit for me after the discussions about discursive meditation (thanks again JMG, Winifred, Jeff and All)!
Here’s the latest after further meditation and study on the types of Samadhi:
Types basically divided into discursive (with conceptualization, or as Judge translated it “argumentation”) or not, either on an object perceived through the senses (gross object) or the more subtle planes all the way up to the Unmanifest.

These four types are said to be “with seed”, that is, all stem from an EFFORT towards gnosis.

Samadhi WITHOUT seed arises once the Yogi is established in their own Self/Soul (or for the Mage, established in the flow of Divine Mind?)

This Samadhi type is then said to be always truth bearing and brings great peace of mind. This Samadhi type is powerful wisdom which keeps the mind clear. Eventually, the mind doesn’t even need this wisdom as all personal desire ceases completely. (A Mage would be in perfect harmony with their Individuality/Higher Self?)

replies.title: Re: Meditative Absorption “East” and “West”
replies.content:
Fortune explained her warnings in detail in some of her books. It wasn't because there was anything wrong with Eastern practices; it was because most Westerners were hopelessly inept at basic spiritual practices, and needed so much remedial work that having them plunge straight into Eastern esotericism was almost a guarantee of total failure.

It's not as valid now as it once was, not least because quite a few competent gurus and teachers reworked the classic Eastern practices to make them suited to total beginners. That said, I've still seen a lot of Western people crash and burn when they plunged enthusiastically into Eastern paths.

replies.title: Re: Ecstatic love
replies.content:
Ramakrishna, after all his experiments in following different mystical paths, was primarily devoted to the Mother, in particular Kali, and gave up unitive mysticism for devotional adoration, saying that he wanted to taste sugar, not be sugar.

Jeffrey Kripal's provocative biography of Ramakrishna claims that there's a strong homoerotic theme in Ramakrishna's mysticism, and life; this has earned him a great deal of hostility from certain quarters in India. Kripal, in other contexts, in part based on his own Catholic seminary experiences, identifies a strong homoerotic strain in Christian mysticism, finding it hard to imagine that passionate love of, and desire for unification with, Jesus, could accord with heterosexual desire. Certainly many (but not all) female mystics have an erotic connection with Jesus, as do a significant number of male mystics (John of the Cross might be a type specimen here). And, even more luridly, a certain period of Moravian mysticism, with the fixation on the "side hole". Elliot Wolfson has an extensive treatment of the same theme in Jewish Kabbalah -- that is, an ostensibly heterosexual erotic mysticism that, to put it hastily, ends up feminizing the make genitalia.

However, there are also female mystics like Dame Julian of Norwich, who experienced Jesus as Mother. And male/male love isn't necessarily sexual: the desire for union can be about not sexual union, but, for example, union with the deity as Hero, or Father, and so on. Finally, desire for union can be an intense desire for union with the Source, the Real, and so on, as in Plotinus, or Meister Eckhart.

When one is dealing with a couple of millennia, and several continents, it's not surprising that, the closer one looks, the more variation one finds, even within traditions that prize orthodoxy.

The Body of Christ is not his remains after crucifixion, but rather, as Paul describes, a metaphysical meta-entity composed of post-ressurection Jesus and all of His followers, operating as a team.

comments.title: Aikido magic questions
comments.content:
JMG,

Two aikido based questions for you today.

1. Most martial arts have a sort of magical founding myth etc. In aikido’s case, there are many stories about Morihei Ueshiba aka O Sensei. In particular, there is a story about his ability to dodge bullets.

The story is relatively recent and well known (from the 1930s) and as well documented as these things could be (one of his young students called Shioda Gozo was an eye witness and later became a famous martial artist in his own right, and told the story many times and even wrote about it in his book, and my own teacher heard it from the Shioda in the early 1990s).

In essence the story is that some well known marksmen heard about his claims/reputation of dodging bullets and invited him to their shooting range if he was serious. O Sensei turned up and stood at the end of the range, by the targets and 5-6 experienced marksmen who could easily hit the targets shot at him. They never hit him and each time they fired at him, they missed, and he somehow got behind them in an instant and was standing behind them laughing (all as witnessed by Shioda Gozo, who had been making preparations to take his Sensei home in a body bag).

Later when they were going home, he asked O Sensei how he’d done it and all O Sensei would say was that as he stood there, he saw glowing balls of light fly from the rifles towards him, but quite slowly, so he could easily dodge them, and he just “knew” that the bullets would follow the same trajectory so he just stayed out of that line and walked towards the marksmen at normal speed and found himself behind them by the time the bullets had been fired.

It is forgotten these days in aikido discussions, but the same book by Shioda Gozo *also* has another story about a different marksman (a famous hunter) who came to O Sensei to do the same thing, and he set himself up and was about to take his shot, when O-Sensei abruptly stood up and said “No no, we cannot do this - I can tell you will hit me, I cannot dodge your bullet” and bowed and acknowledged him as a superior marksman.

If it’s all a fraud why would he do that?

I guess my question is - do you think this kind of thing is magically feasible? That he could have had what I can only describe as magical powers and if so, how? Have there been (reasonably well documented) cases of these kinds of physical powers? Not just bullet dodging but what I can only describe as superpowers?

2. Continuing the aikido theme, here is an excerpt of an aikido essay by a well known (modern day) aikido sensei (a Westerner, but who lived in Japan as a student of Shioda Gozo in the 1980s/90s):

Here he is describing being in pain in a stress position while waiting/watching a lesson and not being allowed to move:

"As it was halfway through the class, my physical agony was already peaking and I had to summon all my self control in order to try and remain fully focused and alert. I did my best, but found it unusually difficult due to an excruciating pain in my lower back that felt like a burning blade twisting between the vertebrae.

Feeling the need to do something, I implemented relaxation techniques that I had learned in yoga, closing my eyes, stretching my spine and focusing on my breathing. I visualized the air as a bright blue vessel that travelled from my lungs to the sore spot, using the breathing pattern to bestow calm as it reached the source of my pain, dispersing tension as it moved away. It took a few minutes, and then, the pain diminished. Inspired by my success, I let the brilliant blue vessel sail to the edges of my trunk, soothingly overriding all discomfort. I inhaled and the brightness expanded away from the borders of the flesh, up through the top of my head and to the heavens, down from the bottom of my spine and merging with the earth.

It felt as if a straight beacon of light was supporting my body, attaching my feet to the ground and at the same time, serving as a perfect launching pad should I decide to move around. I smiled to myself as it suddenly dawned on me that I had never felt so centered before.”

And then later in the same essay:

"The blue light became my obsession during the following days. It was present when I practiced, walked, rode on the train and even as I lay in bed before sleep, the light projecting from my forehead to the ceiling. It seemed like there was no limit to the way it could be applied. It taught me endless lessons, brought fresh ideas into my mind and at the same time, freed me from the shackles of frustration that had bound me for such a long time.

I learned how to connect the spine to my limbs, and how to connect my limbs to the center of my training partners. It also made me very aware of the line on which I moved, and the planes through which power was applied on me or by me. In my inspired mood, I created tens of training methods.

'Power walking,' for example, was one of my favorites, a drill not only enjoyable, but practical too, especially when maneuvering through crowded places. I deployed it on numerous occasions as I came off the train and walked the underground passes of Shinjuku station, drawing an imaginary line toward my desired exit and then charging forward, my back straight and my eyes focused on my goal, keeping to my path, my intent parting the hordes of people that ceaselessly crisscrossed the tightly packed space, like Moses parting the Red Sea.”


My question: This all sounds an awful lot like magic and the kind of thing you talk about in terms of magical rituals? In particular, I am pretty sure I’ve seen you mention some kind of a magical ritual (don’t know if it is a “ritual” as such or simply a magic based skill) - something about imagining yourself as big and powerful and projecting that energy to walk through a crowd and people will clear space for you? Is he basically doing magic here?

replies.title: Re: Aikido magic questions
replies.content:
This question has come before on MMs. It is illuminating to consider that this backfired badly for the Chinese during the Boxer Rebellions.

My thoughts on the subject, now illuminated by your description of O Sensei's antics, tells me that the spell is an altered state of consciousness, shared by the martial artist and the shooters. From the M.A. side, he perceives the "intention to shoot" from the adversary as a very physical, very accurate manifestation of the astral plane, so he can correctly foretell where each bullet is going to land. On the shooter's perspective, it is an elongated perception of time itself, that manifest as a noticeable delay between the "aim" and "shoot" steps of firing a gun. I think O Sensei's tall tale about "appearing" behind the shooters' backs is an exaggeration of a very real "cannot believe how fast this guy moves" perception. That perception is incorrect, too. He probably walked at normal speed (with great care not to get into anybodie's line of fire) while the shooters just kept firing at where he had been 1 or 2 seconds before.

The disaster during the Boxer's Rebellion was that the British did fire in volleys. In the past,jmg has speculated that groups of fighters tightly clustered would have received bullets that were aimed at a companion that stood 4ft to the right or so. My own take is that the British soldiers were already in an altered state of mind (moving and fighting as a platoon), and even if their firing intentions would have been transparent to the Chinese warriors, their commanding officer voice would have short-circuit the time delay of the spell and force them to pull the trigger automatically. The Chinese would have found they had too little time to dodge, even if they could correctly predict the trajectory of the bullets. In any case, the area effect of the volleys did not made things easier for them.

In the case of the Hunter, on the other hand, he had experience ambushing and killing animals that are much more perceptive and agile than us. He was probably very good at obscuring his "intention to kill", and had developed his own techniques to "aim towards the place the animal will move next". With either of this, even if the spell worked correctly and messed with the Hunter's perception of time, Sensei O realized there was too big a risk for himself to walk into the line of fire and receive a bullet that would had missed if he stayed put. In a life or death combat, he might have been able to pull it off; but being a friendly demonstration the wise path was to concede and not to try.

replies.title: Re: Aikido magic questions
replies.content: There is at least one old movie, apparently unretouched, in which Ueshiba moves out from between two big guys who are running at him from opposite sides; he apparently covers about six feet between one frame and the next, and the two big guys slam face first into each other. I have no idea how that happened, or even if that happened, but I'm not prepared to insist that it couldn't have happened.

replies.title: Re: Aikido magic questions
replies.content:
Fascinating!!! If you cannot tell, how could I? Off course there are countless speculations of how could that have happened, but until a modern practitioner rediscovers the technique, it is all chit chat.

I like the "elongated time hypothesis" because it is something I have experienced personally (not as a result of training, but triggered spontaneously by a near car crash) and because it's part of the angelology doctrine I have studied (Evo-time has direction and continuity, but not a single unit of measure). But then again, it is not incompatible with the ability to move your physical body much faster than what's usually considered natural).

replies.title: Re: Aikido magic questions
replies.content: Ueshiba was an initiate of quite a range of old Japanese esoteric traditions -- many of the koryu, the old martial arts schools from the age of wars, had magical and esoteric dimensions. Some of those involve things that seem at least as improbable as these. It's worth remembering that all we have at the present time are small fragments of magical traditions that once contained much, much more.

comments.title: Social Skills
comments.content:
I am curious about your suggestion to "join the Masons or some other such group" as a way to improve social skills. I wouldn't mind improving my social skills although their weakness comes from my dysfunctional upbringing rather than neurodivergence. 2. What kind of group falls into the category of "some other such group?"

Eclectic Pollywog

replies.title: Re: Social Skills
replies.content:
First, gender segregation is a very useful thing in its place. I know a lot of women, for example, who get quite a bit of benefit from women-only groups and women-only spaces. I find the same benefits from men-only groups and spaces such as the Masons. Your mileage may vary but the current prejudice against such groups may not be helpful.

Second, old-fashioned initiatory orders have explicit codes of conduct for their members and a narrowly focused range of activities. For people on the autism spectrum like me, one of the things that's most frustrating about interacting with people is that there's a whole galaxy of rules that most people pick up by nonverbal communication. People on the spectrum don't perceive nonverbal communication! In a lodge, by contrast, the rules are spelled out in so many words. Lodges also value skills like the ability to memorize long passages of text, which most autists are good at, so it's a great confidence builder.

Third, other old-fashioned initiatory lodges such as the Odd Fellows, the Grange, the Knights of Pythias, and so on.

comments.title: SoP vs. LBRP/MP
comments.content:
JMG,

You've said that the Sphere of Protection is a much different ritual (and subtler) than the LBRP & MP. After years gallifaury and pastiche half-assery, I recently decided to commit to magic as the priority in my life, and have been doing the LBRP & MP every day for a month (followed by at least 30 mins of zazen, because I am somewhat involved with the neighboring Soto Zen monastery). Being a writer and artist I think I am a pretty good visualizer, but admittedly I haven't felt all that much doing them. Still, I credit that with some macro changes happening in my life when I look back.

However, I've been god-awful ill with the flu the last few days, and have been soaking up your material. Simply imagining doing the Sphere of Protection while I was reading your instructions in my bed (I can't remember if the version from FHR or DMH) was an EXTREMELY powerful experience for me - nearly brought to tears with joy and gratitude at the first invocation of air.

After reading the FHR materials, the GD stuff makes much more sense to me, and the FHR in its simplicity and the way its laid out resonates so strongly with me. How would you recommend incorporating the SoP into the beginnings of GD initiation work? You say they are very different rituals, but to me (albeit a beginner with more intellectual study than practice) the SoP seems to correspond rather well to the LBRP & MP. Something also doesn't feel quite right about the GD stuff - whether it's because the GD stuff has blinds in it (intentionally, perhaps, or even unintentionally -- you've said GD tended to overemphasize the Tiphareth center, and/or the minds of the men who started it may have been blinded by their own too-solar culture?) or perhaps it has more to do with what jives with my personality and background as a cowboy, I don't know. Or maybe the GD stuff is off because of the way its been reconstructed from Regardie's notes, rather than direct instruction on the original rituals?

(Reading about the Wheel of Life in the Druid Magic Handbook also made the Tree of Life actually click better for me -- and I have a theory that the Tree of Life is just a more "solar" [in the sense of linear, masculine] representation of the Wheel of Life, though I think you would tell me they are distinctly different and incompatible -- I was surprised to see, for example, your Celtic Golden Dawn book doesn't use the Wheel of Life in place of the Tree of Life, which is what I would have imagined works better. Of course, I am very new to the Druid stuff, and probably missing something obvious here.)

What would you say the differences are in the effects of the SoP vs LBRP/MP? How might one go about initiating into Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose as well as HoGD? Why did John Gilbert dispense with the pentagrams and hexagrams of the GD? The other main person I trust in this sphere is Damien Echols, who insists on even beginners just doing the pentagram, hexagram, Rose Cross, and Middle Pillar exercises at least twice every day for a good while (though Cicero suggests the hexagram rituals aren't necessary until the Inner Order) which is I think why I'm a little hesitant to focus on just the FHR for now, even though it resonates with me much more. Would it make sense to do the FHR work once through during the Neophyte phase of GD initiation, for example adding in the SoP either before/after each of my twice daily LBRP/MP rituals? And lastly - what do you think about beginners doing the hexagram and RC exercises, as Damien suggests? For what it's worth, I do have a solid foundation in meditation and visualization (and about 7 years of being a solitary cowboy, it's own kind of meditation & earth connection) -- but I'm sure many beginners think their britches are bigger than they actually are.

Oh, and an unrelated question -- I saw some kind of Being in the grass a few weeks ago. It seemed to me almost inanimate, yet sentient -- like a tall pillar, solid, with slightly billowing white robes. Electric waves and goosebumps went through my body when I directed my attention towards it. Could only see it in my periphery, and I wasn't consciously meditating when I saw it. I had been showering just before, listening to a Why Files video about Atlantis and Lemuria (which I had never heard of before). I went back to the barn and my dog kept trying to go over to it (I couldn't see it at this point as there were trees between me and the barn) and when she came back she was crunching on something -- I had this feeling it had given her a treat or something. I was a bit freaked out at that point and went to bed and pressed play to finish watching the video, and not 15 seconds later, the video showed a picture of the Lemurians (AI generated, I'm sure) he described as 7 foot tall beings cloaked from head to toe in white robes -- EXACTLY like I had seen. The next day, I was reading an old blog post from the monastery next door, and they quote Dogen Zenji's Mountains & Rivers sutra where he talks about the wise sages entering into and living inside the mountains here (Hudson Valley). Any thoughts on this?

Thanks for your time and all your work - I appreciate it more than you know. I hope I got this in on time -- it is 11:18pm here on the East coast, not 2:37 as the page generation seems to say.

- Hayes William

replies.title: Re: SoP vs. LBRP/MP
replies.content:
I really don't recommend mixing and matching. If you're going to work with the Golden Dawn system you need to use the whole system, including the LBRP. If you prefer to use the SoP, you need to use one of the systems that rely on it, such as the FHR. Trying to patch something together out of both is like trying to include truck parts in an airplane, or vice versa -- it's just not going to work well. Choose one system, stick with it, and then once you've completed the full set of training, if you like, you can do the other. (I've done this with close to a dozen systems now!)

As for the figure, that's fascinating -- such things happen, and upstate New York is legendary for them. (There's a reason both Spiritualism and the Mormon church came out of upstate New York.) See if it happens again, and if it does, try communicating with the being.

comments.content: Not sure if this is in under the wire across datelines etc... To what extent are Crowley's translations of the 'barbarous names' in his version of the Bornless Ritual in Liber Samekh accurate or at least credible?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You're 26 minutes ahead of the deadline. Crowley's interpretations of the barbarous names are a fine example of the sort of Cabalistic analysis that's meaningful to the person who does it but gibberish in historical and linguistic terms. That doesn't make it useless in magical practice, far from it, but it's not valid in any other sense.
